points.text: Set client timeout lower than downstream SLA to fail fast and avoid long hangs.
Implement exponential backoff with random jitter for retries to spread load.
Limit max retry attempts and use a circuit breaker to stop calls after repeated failures.
Make retry operations idempotent or use safe HTTP methods (GET, PUT with versioning).
Monitor upstream latency and error rates; adjust timeout dynamically based on percentiles.
On the network side, check for packet loss, congestion, and TCP retransmission spikes using ping, traceroute, and netstat.
Verify TCP window size and enable TCP selective acknowledgments to reduce retransmissions.
Ensure proper MTU settings to avoid fragmentation; use path MTU discovery.
Use exponential backoff with jitter and cap retries to avoid thundering herd during downstream slowness.
Set separate read and write timeouts based on measured latency percentiles of the dependency.
Implement a circuit breaker that opens after a failure threshold and backs off before allowing traffic again.
Propagate deadlines via context and cancel in‑flight calls as soon as the overall request timeout expires.
Profile the latency distribution of the downstream service to size timeout budgets with a safety margin.
On the network, monitor packet loss and congestion; high TCP retransmissions often indicate loss or buffer saturation.
Check load balancer health‑check intervals and TCP keepalive settings to ensure stale connections are cleared.
Tune TCP retransmission timeout (RTO) and max retransmits to reduce long‑lasting retries on flaky links.
Use connection pooling and keep‑alive sockets to avoid repeated TCP handshakes during bursts.
Instrument queue lengths and back‑pressure signals to detect when upstream load exceeds downstream capacity.
Set upstream request timeout shorter than client timeout so failures are detected early.
Introduce a circuit‑breaker that opens after N consecutive failures to stop further retries.
Use exponential backoff with random jitter for retry intervals to spread load spikes.
Cap the maximum number of concurrent retries per downstream service to prevent overload.
Align load‑balancer idle‑timeout and request‑timeout with the service’s own timeout settings.
Monitor load‑balancer queue depth; increase queue size or add capacity if queues regularly fill.
Check Linux kernel TCP retry settings (net.ipv4.tcp_retries2, net.ipv4.tcp_syn_retries) for reasonable limits.
Enable and tune TCP keepalive (keepalive_time, keepalive_intvl) to detect dead peers quickly.
Collect retransmission and packet‑loss metrics via netstat -s or ss -i during incidents.
Verify MTU and avoid fragmentation on the path, as mismatched MTU can cause retransmissions.
Set client timeout to >99th‑percentile RTT plus processing margin to avoid premature retries.
Use exponential backoff with jitter for retries to spread load and prevent retry storms.
Cap retry attempts and implement a circuit‑breaker that opens after N failures to stop cascading retries.
Prefer idempotent operations or request deduplication so retries don’t cause duplicate side effects.
Enable TCP keep‑alives on long‑lived connections to detect dead peers early and free sockets.
Monitor TCP retransmission counters (RTO, RTO‑min) per endpoint to spot rising packet loss or congestion.
Collect RTT variance and smoothed RTT (SRTT) to adjust timeout dynamically based on network conditions.
Check for ECN marks or congestion window reductions indicating network congestion during incidents.
Inspect interface queues and bufferbloat (e.g., using ping‑pong or fq_codel) that can inflate latency and cause timeouts.
Verify path MTU and fragmentation issues that may trigger retransmissions on large payloads.
Budget total request latency (e.g., 500 ms) and allocate per‑dependency timeouts using the p95 latency of each service.
Set per‑dependency timeout slightly above its p95 (e.g., p95 + 10 ms) to avoid premature aborts while limiting tail latency.
Cap retries to 1–2 attempts and use exponential backoff (e.g., 100 ms, 200 ms) to prevent retry storms.
Implement a circuit‑breaker that opens after N failures within a short window to stop cascading retries.
Measure TCP RTT, loss, and retransmission counts; high loss or RTT spikes indicate network congestion causing timeouts.
Enable TCP selective acknowledgments (SACK) and tune the retransmission timeout (RTO) to be adaptive to observed RTT variance.
Monitor queue lengths and use Little’s Law (L = λ · W) to ensure request arrival rate stays below service capacity under timeout budgets.
